senses.definition: (of a train) to travel along a track on wheels, carrying passengers or goods

examples.x: Yesterday, the train ran smoothly despite the stormy weather that disrupted other modes of transportation.

senses.definition: to move a train or part of a train from one track to another

examples.x: The engineer had to shunt the freight cars to a different track.
The signalman instructed the crew to shunt the train to the sideline.
The operator carefully shunted the train to avoid any collisions.
They used a locomotive to shunt the passenger carriages into the station.
She watched as the workers shunted the empty cars to make room for new cargo.

senses.definition: (of a train or bus) to arrive at a station

examples.x: The train pulled in right on time.
I'll meet you as soon as my bus pulls in.
Passengers were alerted as the train was about to pull in to the terminal.
As the sun rose, the first train of the day pulled in.
He was on the platform when the midnight train pulled in.

senses.definition: (of a train or bus) to leave a station with passengers on board

examples.x: The 9 o'clock train pulled out right on time.
As I reached the platform, the bus had already pulled out.
Everyone was seated and the express train pulled out, heading to its next destination.
He missed his stop because he dozed off, waking up only after the bus had pulled out.
She watched from the window as the countryside passed by after the train pulled out.

senses.definition: (of a train) to accidentally go off the tracks

examples.x: The heavy rain and slippery tracks led to a tragic incident as the express train derailed.
In the midst of a fierce snowstorm, a commuter train derailed.
A mechanical failure in one of the freight train's wheels caused it to derail.
A freight train carrying goods derailed in a remote area.

senses.definition: to disconnect two railway cars or a car from the locomotive

examples.x: The crew had to uncouple the last two cars for maintenance.
They needed to uncouple the engine to perform repairs on it.
The workers uncoupled the dining car before sending the rest of the train to the yard.
He used the lever to uncouple the freight car from the rest of the train.
She signaled the conductor to uncouple the caboose from the main train.

senses.definition: a directive issued to reduce speed for safety reasons on a railway track

examples.x: The train conductor received a slow order due to construction work ahead on the tracks.
Passengers were informed of the slow order, which extended the travel time by twenty minutes.
The railway authorities imposed a temporary slow order following heavy rainfall to prevent potential track damage.
Despite the inconvenience, adhering to the slow order ensured the safety of everyone on board.
The locomotive's speed decreased as it approached the designated slow order zone near the station.

senses.definition: the practice of attaching two locomotives at the front of a train to provide additional power for hauling heavy loads or climbing steep gradients

examples.x: In soccer, players often use double heading to redirect the ball towards the goal from two different angles.
The company's strategy involved a double heading approach: expanding their current market while exploring new opportunities abroad.
The bookshelf was mounted with a double heading, allowing books to be stored facing both inward and outward.
The political speech aimed to achieve a double heading effect, appealing to both urban and rural voters simultaneously.
The design of the aircraft's cockpit featured a double heading display, providing pilots with crucial information from multiple viewpoints.

senses.definition: a device or mechanism that operates without control or restraint, often resulting in dangerous situations
a safety device placed at the end of a railway track to prevent trains from moving beyond that point

examples.x: The buffer stop ensures that trains come to a safe and controlled halt at the end of the track.
Engineers regularly inspect and maintain buffer stops to guarantee their effectiveness in railway operations.
When a train approaches the buffer stop, it activates braking systems automatically to avoid collisions.
Buffer stops are designed to absorb impact energy in case of accidental overruns, minimizing damage to both trains and infrastructure.
In some cases, buffer stops are equipped with sensors to alert railway operators of any potential issues or obstructions on the track.

senses.definition: a rotating platform used to redirect train engines or cars between different tracks

examples.x: The railway turntable allows trains to change direction easily without needing to use multiple tracks.
Engineers carefully position locomotives on the railway turntable before rotating them to align with the desired track.
In the past, railway turntables were manually operated, but modern ones are often mechanized for efficiency.
Maintenance crews regularly inspect and lubricate the railway turntable to ensure smooth operation.
A railway turntable is a crucial component in train yards where space for maneuvering trains is limited.

senses.definition: a braking system used on a rail yard track to slow down or stop rolling railroad cars with controlled friction or electromagnetic force

examples.x: The railroad yard employs sophisticated retarders to safely manage the speed of incoming freight cars.
A powerful electromechanical retarder is essential for precise control over the positioning of railcars during switching operations.
Operators adjust the retarder settings based on the weight and speed of the cars to prevent collisions and ensure efficient sorting.
Regular maintenance of the retarder system is crucial to maintain operational safety and reliability in the rail yard.
Engineers are constantly exploring innovations in retarder technology to enhance efficiency and reduce energy consumption in rail operations.

senses.definition: a safety device on machinery that stops it from operating if the operator becomes incapacitated or loses control
the pulling or hauling force exerted by a locomotive or vehicle

examples.x: The steam engine's tractive effort enabled it to pull heavy loads across long distances.
Modern electric trains rely on their powerful motors for high tractive effort, ensuring efficient movement of freight.
Engineers often calculate the tractive effort needed based on the weight of the train and the gradient of the track.
In mountainous regions, trains require more tractive effort to ascend steep inclines.
Diesel locomotives are known for their robust tractive effort, making them suitable for both freight and passenger services.

senses.definition: the event where two trains traveling on the same track come together

examples.x: Train meets are carefully scheduled to prevent accidents on busy rail routes.
The train meet at the station caused a slight delay in departure times.
Passengers on board felt a slight jolt as the train meet occurred unexpectedly.
The railway authorities emphasize the importance of signaling systems to ensure safe train meets.
Understanding the timetable is crucial to avoid missing a train meet at the junction.

senses.definition: a system where locomotives and carriages are controlled simultaneously from a single point within the train

examples.x: Multiple-unit train control coordinates locomotives and carriages for efficient long-distance travel.
Engineers use multiple-unit train control for synchronized braking and acceleration, ensuring safety and reliability.
Implementing multiple-unit train control has reduced manpower and improved service in rail transport.
Passengers experience smoother rides with multiple-unit train control during busy times.
Maintenance crews rely on multiple-unit train control diagnostics to prevent mechanical issues and reduce delays.

senses.definition: a system used in rail operations to authorize train movements along specific sections of track based on direct communication between train crews and dispatchers

examples.x: Effective communication between dispatchers and crews is crucial in track warrant control.
Track warrant control authorizes trains to move safely on designated track sections.
Track warrant control allows for flexible scheduling of train movements based on real-time conditions.
Train crews rely on track warrant control for clear guidance on their route and operational restrictions.
During maintenance or emergencies, track warrant control facilitates quick adjustments to train routes and schedules.

senses.definition: a system that automatically regulates the speed and movement of trains to ensure safety and efficiency

examples.x: Many modern railways employ automatic train control to prevent collisions and manage traffic flow.
The implementation of automatic train control has significantly reduced the risk of human error in train operations.
Engineers continually refine automatic train control systems to integrate new technologies and improve performance.
Passengers benefit from automatic train control through smoother rides and reduced travel times.
Governments around the world increasingly mandate the use of automatic train control to enhance railway safety standards.

senses.definition: a safety system installed on railways to prevent accidents caused by human error

examples.x: Automatic train protection ensures that trains automatically slow down or stop if they exceed speed limits, enhancing passenger safety.
In the event of a signal passed at danger, automatic train protection activates emergency brakes to prevent collisions.
Many modern railways worldwide have adopted automatic train protection to minimize the risk of accidents and improve operational reliability.
The implementation of automatic train protection has significantly reduced the occurrence of accidents related to speeding or signal violations on railway networks.
The introduction of automatic train protection has standardized safety measures across different railway networks, ensuring consistent passenger security.

senses.definition: a system that allows trains to operate without constant manual intervention from a driver

examples.x: Many modern metro systems around the world employ automatic train operation to ensure efficient and safe transportation.
Passengers often appreciate the smooth and predictable travel experience provided by automatic train operation.
